item_17_comment: Tutor: ~75–80%, Adam: ~20–25%. Tutor talks a lot while modeling and guiding; Adam answers many questions and narrates some actions but less overall.

item_18_comment: 1 of 1 students actively involved (100%). Adam spoke frequently, well over two substantial turns.

item_19_comment: Rough estimate: ~40% of feedback was specific (e.g., "bardzo dobre odpowiedzi. Świetnie zapamiętałeś", comments on correct use of dimensions, anchoring, grouping), ~60% was more generic praise or encouragement ("Super", "Dobrze", "Bardzo fajnie").

item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Roughly 45% prompts required thinking/reasoning (explaining 1D/2D/3D, deciding materials, planning structures, locating functions in UI), 55% were procedural/technical (click here, open this, duplicate that, login steps).

item_21_comment: Significant time is spent on technical issues (audio, Zoom, Codland login, screen sharing), but there is also substantial instructional time on coordinates, agent, and coding. Approx. 60% productive learning (content, practice, guided coding), 40% technical/admin/troubleshooting.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts require conceptual thinking (explaining Minecraft Education, coordinates, planning movements, using agent logic), but a large share are procedural (click here, share screen, enter password). Roughly 40% thinking/reasoning prompts, 60% procedural/technical prompts.

item_6_examples: Gra z prawda/fałsz liczbami – nauczycielka czyta liczby, wspólnie sprawdzają poprawność zanim dzieci robią podobne zadania samodzielnie. | Przy porównywaniu liczb na koralikach: szczegółowe omówienie trzech kroków porównywania z przykładami 4 265 i 3 256. | Przy zadaniach tekstowych (zapałki, węzły, kłoda, latarnie, zegar) – rysuje schemat, włącza "żaróweczkę" z podpowiedzią i przechodzi przez równania zanim przejdą do samodzielnej pracy na platformie.
item_7_question: Student Engagement (Names) Score 1 only if: - tutor calls at least one student by name more than 2 times during the lesson
item_7_why: Imiona uczniów były używane wielokrotnie w całej lekcji, każde dziecko było wielokrotnie wywoływane po imieniu.
item_7_examples: "Sonia" pojawia się dziesiątki razy (np. "Sonia: większa będzie 1 482.", "Sonia: 1 075.") | "Nadia"/"Nadusia" wielokrotnie ("Nadusia: większy bankiet. 8 734.") | "Krzysiu", "Franek", "Roxy" również powtarzane wielokrotnie.
item_8_question: Student Engagement (Participation) Score 1 only if tutor directly invites student participation (answer/read/explain/share idea)
item_8_why: Tutor bardzo często zapraszał uczniów do aktywnego udziału: odpowiadania, rozwiązywania przykładów, zgłaszania się, udostępniania ekranu.
item_8_examples: "poproszę cię o pierwszy Przykład która liczba większa." | "poproszę. Drugi przykład.", "kto ma ochotę rozwiązać" ("kto ma hotel rozwiązać.") | "poproszę o zgłaszanie się. Podnoszenie rąk" oraz liczne indywidualne pytania: "Krzysiu, co ty na to?", "Franek, żebyś też odpowiedział coś."
item_9_question: Broad and Inclusive Participation Did the tutor actively involve multiple students rather than relying on one active child?
item_9_why: Udział był rozdzielany między wielu uczniów; tutor świadomie włączał różne osoby, także te mniej aktywne.
item_9_examples: Kolejne zadania rozdzielane między Sonię, Nadię, Roxy, Krzysia i Franka (np. seria porównań liczb, zadania tekstowe, przykłady z odległościami). | Tutor celowo zwraca się do mniej mówiących: "Franek, Ee! Ja, bym cię poprosiła. Też teraz o aktywność tutaj na tablicy", "to może krzysiu. Ty też może sobie w sumie 2 przykłady zrobić."
item_10_question: Student Engagement (Share Screen / Work) Did the tutor ask students to share their screen OR show their work (e.g., "Share your screen", "Show me what you did", "Please share it with me", "Share your project", "Share your drawing")?
item_10_why: Tutor wielokrotnie prosił uczniów o udostępnianie ekranu i pokazywanie pracy na platformie.
item_10_examples: "udostępniajcie ekrany też od razu." | "Franciszek: Udało mi się. Laura: Dobra. Super! To udostępniaj ekran." | "Dobra, to sonia. W takim razie poproszę o udostępnienie Krem, z tym Kodlanem."
item_11_question: Did the tutor explicitly ask at least one student to turn their camera on (or to keep it on) in order to support attention/participation during the lesson?
item_11_why: Były wyraźne prośby o włączenie kamer, zarówno indywidualne jak i do całej grupy, w celu lepszej pracy.
item_11_examples: Na początku do Soni: "Możemy się zobaczyć też włączyłabyś kamerę." | Do grupy: "a możemy się zobaczyć w ogóle. To też jest prośba do całej grupy... ale bym chciała was widzieć. Słuchajcie Roxy, i nadia i krzysiu."
item_12_question: Content delivery: Did the tutor use the platform (e.g., Kodland) or other visual tools to explain assignments or tasks?
item_12_why: Cała część zadaniowa była prowadzona na platformie Kodland / prezentacji z liskiem; tutor używał tych wizualnych narzędzi do wyjaśniania i ćwiczeń.
item_12_examples: "Dobra, widzimy moją prezentację." – następnie opis slajdów z liskiem i celami. | "pojawił się na czacie link ode mnie i tam wpisujemy swój login z platformy" oraz późniejsze monitorowanie pracy na Kodlandzie. | Pod koniec: "Tutaj macie moja trochę inaczej. Ta platforma wygląda. Ale jest taka zakładka. Zadania domowe" – pokazuje interfejs.
item_13_question: Lesson Wrap-Up (Tutor-led) In the final segment of the lesson (last ~25 minutes), did the tutor deliver a content-based wrap-up by summarizing what was learned or accomplished?
item_13_why: W końcowej części lekcji tutor jasno podsumował, czego się nauczyli, wymieniając konkretne treści.
item_13_examples: "A dzisiaj nauczyliśmy się porównywać liczby 3 i czterocyfrowe. Określiliśmy, która liczba, jest bliższa w danej liczbie utrwaliliśmy umiejętności rozwiązywania zadań tekstowych."
item_14_question: Lesson Wrap-Up (Student-led) In the final segment of the lesson (last ~25 minutes) did the tutor ask students to summarize, reflect on, discuss the lesson, or participate in a revision / summary quiz in the final part of the lesson?
item_14_why: Na końcu uczniowie byli proszeni o refleksję, co im się podobało, i odpowiadali pełnymi wypowiedziami.
item_14_examples: "Pogadajmy, co wam się dzisiaj podobało w zajęciach." | Nadia: "Wszystko.", Roxy: "Wszystko mi się podobało. No i nie było żadnej chwili.", Sonia: "To, że mogliśmy robić same ćwiczenia..."
item_15_question: Homework for the Next Class (Lesson Structure / Continuity) Score 1 only if tutor clearly describes what students must do at home (task details). Merely mentioning “homework exists” = 0.Clear and Meaningful Next Step
item_15_why: Zostało jasno opisane zadanie domowe: gdzie je znaleźć i że jest to quiz/utrwalenie dzisiejszego materiału.
item_15_examples: "na Kodlandzie, macie zadanie domowe w zakładce zadania domowe." | "To jest takie fajne utrwalenie tego, co dzisiaj się działo... macie właśnie taki quiz taką grę wam pomoże utrwalić tą wiedzę."
item_16_question: Upcoming Lesson (Lesson Structure / Continuity) Score 1 only if tutor clearly states the topic/activity of the next lesson (“Next class we will…”). “See you next week” without topic = 0.
item_16_why: Tutor wyraźnie zapowiedział temat kolejnej lekcji.
item_16_examples: "na następnej Lekcji Słuchajcie! Nauczymy, się dodawać i odejmować duże liczby. Rozwiążemy zagadki logiczne, od lista i wszystko utrwalimy dzięki zadaniom z treścią"
item_17_question: Comment: Talk Balance (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate the ratio of tutor talk vs. student talk during the lesson. Return as percentages, for example: "Tutor: 74%, Student1: 14%, Student2: 12%"
item_17_comment: Tutor: ok. 70–75% (długie wyjaśnienia, instrukcje, zarządzanie techniczne). Sonia: ~10–12%. Nadia: ~8–10%. Roxy: ~5–7%. Franek: ~5–7%. Krzysiu: ~3–5%. Ogólnie dominacja mowy nauczyciela, ale z częstymi krótkimi odpowiedziami uczniów.
item_18_question: Comment: Student Engagement Rate (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of students actively engaged (spoke more than twice)?
item_18_comment: 5 z 5 obecnych uczniów (Sonia, Nadusia, Roksi, Krzysiu, Franciszek) mówili więcej niż dwa razy – 100% aktywnie zaangażowanych.
item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Około 70% pochwał było dość ogólnych ("super", "ekstra", "pięknie"), ok. 30% zawierało element konkretu (np. "czyli właśnie ta ósemka jest większa niż jedynkę, prawda?", "bardzo dobrze, że myślisz o dzieleniu").
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Szacunkowo ok. 30% pytań było bardziej otwartych/wyjaśniających ("Skąd o tym wiesz?", "Jaki masz pomysł?", "Jakie równanie powinniśmy zrobić?"), a ok. 70% to pytania zamknięte/proceduralne (wybór liczby, prawda/fałsz, techniczne o kamerze/ekranie).
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Około 85–90% czasu to aktywne uczenie się (wyjaśnienia, wspólne rozwiązywanie, praca na platformie, refleksja). Około 10–15% to kwestie techniczne (kamery, udostępnianie ekranu, logowanie) i krótkie przerwy/organizacja.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Około 55–60% poleceń wymagało myślenia/rozumowania (porównywanie liczb, zadania tekstowe z odległościami, wyjaśnianie strategii), a 40–45% było czysto proceduralnych (kliknij, udostępnij ekran, wpisz login, wybierz odpowiedź bez uzasadnienia).

item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts required reasoning about code, debugging, and explaining concepts (conditionals, loops, random, time). Some were procedural ("compárteme pantalla", "entra al módulo 3"). Approx: 65% thinking/reasoning prompts, 35% procedural/technical.

item_19_comment: Aproximadamente 30% del feedback fue específico (por ejemplo, corrige o amplía la respuesta sobre input, comenta metas y progreso hacia el videojuego); el resto (~70%) fueron comentarios generales como "Vale perfecto", "Vamos bien encaminados", "los felicito" sin detalle concreto.

item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Alrededor de 85% del tiempo fue productivo (explicación de comandos, trabajo en actividades, demostración del Hub, Kahoot, resumen y reflexión). Aproximadamente 15% se dedicó a temas técnicos (acceso a plataforma, contraseña, recargar página) y pequeñas pausas (ir al baño).
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Aproximadamente 45% de las intervenciones del tutor requerían pensamiento o razonamiento (explicar metas, recordar contenidos, aplicar comandos en proyectos, responder preguntas conceptuales). El 55% restante fueron instrucciones procedimentales o técnicas (cómo entrar a actividades, tiempos, navegación de plataforma, preguntas de sí/no sobre claridad).

item_1_examples: "Supercje. Widzieć, jak ci minął tydzień. Fajnie." | "jak ci minął Tydzień? Albert, dobrze." | "To się bardzo cieszę coś ciekawego. Się wydarzyło, że tak fajnie minął."
item_2_question: Lesson Objectives In the early part of the lesson (first ~ 25 minutes) did the tutor clearly state the goals of the lesson before starting the practical part of the lesson (e.g., "Today we will learn…", "Today we will discuss…", "In this lesson we are going to explore…", "By the end of this lesson you will be able to…", "The lesson is about…", "The lesson will be about…", "The lesson is dedicated to…", "Today we will cover…")?
item_2_why: Objectives are clearly stated near the start, before practical coding begins.
item_2_examples: "Dziś wyruszamy na kolejną ekscytującą przygodę... Czeka kolejna misja" | "Musimy powtórzyć to, czego się nauczyliśmy podczas ostatniej lekcji" | "Dzięki naszej kreatywności w połączeniu z mocą Scratch Junior możemy z łatwością zbudować rakietę i narysować kosmiczną scenę."
item_3_question: Revision of the Previous Class Did the tutor ask the students to recall what they studied in the previous class by actively involving them at the beginning or early part of the lesson?
item_3_why: Tutor explicitly prompts recall of previous lesson and students respond about content.
item_3_examples: "Musimy powtórzyć to, czego się nauczyliśmy podczas ostatniej lekcji, czyli co było na wyspie 2." | Quiz questions about prior content: "Control, plus, C! Co robi? Ten skrót klawiszowy." | Student answers: "Ko kopiuję.", "Co to jest sprite." – "Nasz duszek wyskradzi."
item_4_question: Homework Review Did the tutor review or ask about the students' home task (even if the word "homework" was not explicitly used), for example by referring to a specific assignment that was meant to be done at home (e.g., "Have you completed your homework assignments?
item_4_why: Tutor refers to an unfinished prior assignment and asks learner to complete it at home, treating it as work to be done between lessons.
item_4_examples: "No to koniecznie po dzisiejszych zajęciach. Dobrze gdyby się odrobiła, ponieważ poznawaliśmy tam nowe bloki." | "Dzisiaj po zajęciach. Też możesz."
item_5_question: Clarity of explanation: Clear and Manageable Instruction Chunking In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor break instructions into manageable, child-friendly chunks?
item_5_why: Instructions are consistently broken into small, sequential, child-friendly steps without overloading students.
item_5_examples: "Czyli domek... I plusik super. I teraz zaczniemy od rakiety. Więc najpierw możemy usunąć Tika." | "Klikamy na Tika, i przytrzymujemy... plusik od razu pędzel i teraz można narysować własną rakietę." | "Najpierw blok startowy flaga i paluszek... Dajemy flagę, powrót na miejsce i z bloków wyglądu bierzemy sobie właśnie tekst."
item_6_question: Clarity of Explanation: Effective Modeling Before Expecting Independence In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor model the task clearly before expecting students to work independently?
item_6_why: Tutor models tasks by walking through examples and showing exact blocks and sequences before expecting independent work.
item_6_examples: Opis narzędzi rysowania: "Możemy korzystać z linii... kółka kwadraty i trójkąty... jeżeli chcemy coś usunąć, to... nożyczki". | Model kodu z prezentacji: "Zobaczcie, mamy kod, kiedy wystartujemy to nasza rakieta napisać ready... później... będzie odlatywać, robić się mniejsza... aż w końcu robimy, żeby jej nie było widać." | Przekładanie tego na Scratch Junior: "Czyli najpierw blok startowy flaga i paluszek... mamy mieć w górę o 2 jednostki... zmniejszenie o 2..."
item_7_question: Student Engagement (Names) Score 1 only if: - tutor calls at least one student by name more than 2 times during the lesson
item_7_why: Tutor repeatedly uses several students’ names throughout the lesson, each more than twice.

item_8_question: Student Engagement (Participation) Score 1 only if tutor directly invites student participation (answer/read/explain/share idea)
item_8_why: Tutor frequently invites students to answer questions, share ideas, and perform actions.
item_8_examples: "Albert, na co głosujesz? Co ten kursor oznacza." | "Marcin jesteś gotowy? No to losuję... Okej? Co to jest sprite." | "Czy wszystko jest jasne, tak." followed by checking and responding to questions about tło and rakieta.
item_9_question: Broad and Inclusive Participation Did the tutor actively involve multiple students rather than relying on one active child?
item_9_why: Tutor actively involves multiple named students (both Alicjas, Albert, Marcin) with directed questions and support, not just one dominant child.
item_9_examples: Losowanie pytań po kolei: pytania do Alicja Seredyn, Albert, Marcin, Alicja Spyra. | Indywidualne wsparcie przy pracy: "Albert, zobacz teraz zadziałało.", "Ok. Teraz dajemy plusik... Marcin...", "Alicja? Też widzę tylko jeszcze prezentację musimy włączyć znowu." | Zapraszanie do prezentacji/kodu: "Marcin jesteś gotowy?", "Alicja Spera, Czy, jesteś gotowa?"
item_10_question: Student Engagement (Share Screen / Work) Did the tutor ask students to share their screen OR show their work (e.g., "Share your screen", "Show me what you did", "Please share it with me", "Share your project", "Share your drawing")?
item_10_why: Tutor explicitly asks students to share their screens and monitors their shared work.
item_10_examples: "Włączam, żeby każdy mógł udostępnić ekran." | "Już patrzę, od kogo mam od alicji... widzę u Alberta? Mam Tak Marcin?" | "prosiłam, żebyś tylko udostępniała znowu ekran"
item_11_question: Did the tutor explicitly ask at least one student to turn their camera on (or to keep it on) in order to support attention/participation during the lesson?
item_11_why: No explicit request to turn cameras on or keep them on for attention/participation.
item_11_examples: "Widzę, że wyłączyła ci kamerkę. Ale już działa."
item_11_instead: Tutor comments on camera status but does not explicitly ask any student or the group to turn cameras on; focus is on screens and internet issues.
item_12_question: Content delivery: Did the tutor use the platform (e.g., Kodland) or other visual tools to explain assignments or tasks?
item_12_why: Tutor uses a presentation and on-screen instructions as visual tools to explain tasks and code.
item_12_examples: "Zatrzymuje udostępnianie wysyłam wam informację..." then refers to instructions and examples. | "Możemy najpierw kliknąć w instrukcję... Mamy wykrzyknik. Zobaczcie, mamy informację, jak dodać własny sprint." | "Zobaczcie, mamy kod, kiedy wystartujemy to nasza rakieta napisać ready" – clearly referencing displayed code on the platform.
item_13_question: Lesson Wrap-Up (Tutor-led) In the final segment of the lesson (last ~25 minutes), did the tutor deliver a content-based wrap-up by summarizing what was learned or accomplished?
item_13_why: In the closing phase tutor summarizes specific skills and concepts learned.
item_13_examples: "Nie tylko pomogliśmy te diamonu zbudować dzisiaj rakietę, ale też pogłębiliśmy naszą wiedzę na temat programowania." | "Nauczyliśmy się tworzyć własne duszki i tła, a nawet dowiedzieliśmy się, jak tworzyć animacje. W skarb Junior."
item_14_question: Lesson Wrap-Up (Student-led) In the final segment of the lesson (last ~25 minutes) did the tutor ask students to summarize, reflect on, discuss the lesson, or participate in a revision / summary quiz in the final part of the lesson?
item_14_why: End-of-lesson wrap-up is tutor-led; students are not asked to reflect or summarize in the final segment.
item_14_examples: Closing is monologic: "Wow? Spójrz na Te, Diego?...", "To tyle na dzisiaj..." with no reflection questions.
item_14_instead: Tutor praises work and summarizes learning but does not invite students to say what they learned, enjoyed, or found difficult, nor run a revision quiz at the end.
item_15_question: Homework for the Next Class (Lesson Structure / Continuity) Score 1 only if tutor clearly describes what students must do at home (task details). Merely mentioning “homework exists” = 0.Clear and Meaningful Next Step
item_15_why: No clear, detailed homework task for next class is assigned at the end.
item_15_examples: Earlier: "No to koniecznie po dzisiejszych zajęciach. Dobrze gdyby się odrobiła" refers to past missed work, not a new assignment. | Closing: "To tyle na dzisiaj... do zobaczenia za tydzień" with no task description.
item_15_instead: Tutor mentions completing a previous missed task and suggests finishing code/tło during lesson, but does not clearly set a specific at-home assignment for all students for next time.
item_16_question: Upcoming Lesson (Lesson Structure / Continuity) Score 1 only if tutor clearly states the topic/activity of the next lesson (“Next class we will…”). “See you next week” without topic = 0.
item_16_why: Tutor states that next lesson will continue Teddi’s adventures and introduce new blocks/skills.
item_16_examples: "na następnej zobaczymy. co się wydarzy, jakie następne przygody ma dla nas tedy, jakie nowe umiejętności, poznamy. Jakie nowe bloki."
item_17_question: Comment: Talk Balance (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate the ratio of tutor talk vs. student talk during the lesson. Return as percentages, for example: "Tutor: 74%, Student1: 14%, Student2: 12%"
item_17_comment: Tutor: ~75–80%. Students combined: ~20–25% (Albert ~8–10%, Alicja Spyra ~6–8%, Alicja Seredyn ~4–6%, Marcin ~4–6%). Tutor leads explanations and guidance; students answer questions and talk during coding but less overall.
item_18_question: Comment: Student Engagement Rate (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of students actively engaged (spoke more than twice)?
item_18_comment: 4 of 4 named students (Albert, Alicja Spyra, Alicja Seredyn, Marcin) spoke multiple times with multi-word turns. 4 of 4 actively involved (100%).
item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Most feedback is specific (e.g., on colors, shapes, code blocks) rather than generic. Approx. 70% specific ("Bardzo fajnie. Wyszedł ten płomień...", "Brakuje tylko jednego bloku u ciebie"), 30% generic praise ("Świetna robota.", "Bardzo dobrze.").
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Tutor asks many content and process questions with short expected answers (closed or narrowly focused) and some open prompts. Roughly 30% open-ended (e.g., "Jak myślisz, Albert.", "Jaką chcecie rakietę"), 70% closed/specific ("Co to jest sprite.", "Co robi ten skrót klawiszowy.", "Czy wszystko jest jasne?").
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: About 85–90% productive learning time (review quiz, instructions, modeling, guided coding, troubleshooting within task). Around 10–15% technical/admin/dead time (internet issues, reconnecting, screen-share setup, brief small talk).
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts are procedural (click here, add this block) but there is also conceptual recall and some design choice. Approx. 40% thinking/reasoning (recall prior lesson, interpret icons, design algorithm for buźka, choose rocket design), 60% procedural/technical (steps in Scratch Junior, sharing screen, where to click).

item_21_comment: Productive learning (coding, conceptual questions, game-based practice, reflection) dominated. Some admin/technical time (login, being kicked, screen issues). Approx. 85–90% productive learning, 10–15% technical/admin.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts required thinking (predicting blocks, counting blocks/backgrounds/sprites, deciding moves, reflecting on tasks). Also many procedural prompts (click here, enter this value). Rough estimate: 55–60% thinking/reasoning prompts, 40–45% procedural/technical.

item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Most feedback was procedural or brief reassurance ("dobrze", "super", "jak najbardziej", confirming things work). Detailed feedback on correctness of code/links appeared but was less frequent. Estimate: ~30% detailed/specific (explaining why something failed, pointing to exact line), ~70% generic or minimal.
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Many questions were open-ended conceptual or diagnostic ("jakim tagiem możemy...", "czy to jest jasne?", "jakieś pytania?"), but also many closed/technical checks ("masz już uruchomione?", "widzisz link?", "czy to działa?"). Estimate: ~45% open-ended, ~55% closed/technical.
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Most of the time was on explaining Flask/Jinja, HTML links, and guiding students through setup and debugging. Some time went to admin (attendance, payment/pause discussion, Git hosting pricing). Estimate: ~85% productive learning, ~15% admin/technical or off-topic.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: A substantial portion required reasoning (understanding dynamic routes, Jinja variables, link construction, interpreting errors). However, a lot was procedural (click here, extract zip, run this command). Estimate: ~55% thinking/reasoning prompts, ~45% procedural/technical prompts.

item_20_comment: Tutor questions were mostly open-ended content/reflective or procedural ("come si fa…?", "cosa potrebbe essere un ciclo?", "cosa ti è piaciuto di più?"). Roughly 70% open-ended vs 30% closed/yes-no or purely technical.
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Productive learning time was high despite some technical friction: about 80% on coding/Minecraft concepts, building, reviewing homework, and reflection; ~20% on technical issues (Zoom, coordinates, controls) and end-of-lesson free play.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts required thinking/explaining (definitions, recalling commands, choosing materials, reflecting on learning), but there was also a lot of step-by-step procedural guidance. Approx. 55% thinking/reasoning vs 45% procedural/technical.

There was some technical dead time (plugin not working, 5-minute break) and a long off-task game segment after class was formally ended. For the scheduled class time: about 75–80% productive learning, 20–25% technical/admin/dead time. If including the voluntary game time, the overall session skews more toward non-instructional in the last 15–20 minutes.

item_17_question: Comment: Talk Balance (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate the ratio of tutor talk vs. student talk during the lesson. Return as percentages, for example: "Tutor: 74%, Student1: 14%, Student2: 12%"
item_17_comment: Tutor talk dominates, with frequent detailed explanations and step-by-step coding guidance. Students respond with short questions and confirmations, plus some dialogue during debugging. Rough estimate: Tutor ~80–85%, Dharma ~5–7%, Fadil ~4–6%, others (Roy/Royce, Darrel, David, Michelle, Dika, hafiz, rois) together ~5–8%.
item_18_question: Comment: Student Engagement Rate (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of students actively engaged (spoke more than twice)?
item_18_comment: Several named students speak multiple times: Dharma and Fadil are highly active; Roy/Royce and possibly one or two others speak more than twice. If class size is around 6–8 active students, about 3–4 are actively engaged. Roughly: 3–4 of 7–8 students actively involved (~40–60%).
item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Most feedback is specific and task-focused (pointing to exact lines, variables, missing parentheses), with little generic praise. Approximate: ~80–85% detailed/specific (e.g., “di baris enam puluh dua nih… kamu belum ada tutup kurungnya”), ~15–20% generic (“Good job nih mas keren”).
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Tutor asks many procedural/closed questions ("paham nggak", "udah", "bisa nggak", "siapa yang udah di nomor empat") and some more open, reasoning-oriented prompts about how to use commands. Rough estimate: ~25–30% open-ended/content questions (e.g., asking Darrel about speed command, asking how to set thickness), ~70–75% closed or procedural/technical questions.
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Most of the time is spent on explaining concepts (speed, width, circle, dot), modeling code, and helping students debug and build avatars. There is some overhead with screensharing and minor technical confusion, but it remains instructional. Rough estimate: ~85–90% productive learning time, ~10–15% technical/admin/dead time (photo, saving/submitting, minor platform issues).
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: A large portion of prompts are procedural (where to click, what to type, fixing syntax), but there are also conceptual prompts about understanding speed values, coordinates, and variable vs string colors. Rough estimate: ~35–40% thinking/reasoning prompts, ~60–65% procedural or technical prompts.

item_1_question: Warm-Up and Greetings Did the tutor greet the students in a friendly and engaging manner and explicitly ask at least one small-talk question about the students' week, school, or holidays (e.g., "How was your week?

item_1_examples: "Allora com'è stato? Ragazzi, Com'è andato." | "Ognuno a turno mi dice un po. Mi racconta un po quello che ha fatto." | Finale: "Ditemi in ordine. Tu vai a cena! Edoardo... tu, Giovanni. Cosa fai?... E tu Emanuele, invece."
item_2_question: Lesson Objectives In the early part of the lesson (first ~ 25 minutes) did the tutor clearly state the goals of the lesson before starting the practical part of the lesson (e.g., "Today we will learn…", "Today we will discuss…", "In this lesson we are going to explore…", "By the end of this lesson you will be able to…", "The lesson is about…", "The lesson will be about…", "The lesson is dedicated to…", "Today we will cover…")?
item_2_why: He names the lesson number and mentions topic elements, but never clearly states an objective in a "today we will learn..." form before starting activities.
item_2_examples: "Allora ragazzi, iniziamo con la lezione. Sono 58 . Partiamo..." | "Allora, intanto, benvenuti alla lezione. 2 ... Allora iniziamo con la lezione. 2 ." | "E ovviamente sì, l'avventura va avanti con la tastiera."
item_2_instead: He transitions directly into content and questions about mouse/tastiera without an explicit learning goal like "oggi impariamo a usare la tastiera" or similar.
item_3_question: Revision of the Previous Class Did the tutor ask the students to recall what they studied in the previous class by actively involving them at the beginning or early part of the lesson?
item_3_why: He explicitly initiates a recap of previous content (mouse) and students respond with prior knowledge.

Io adesso vi faccio delle domande." | "Parto con la prima domanda: ragazzi, a cosa Serve il mouse." | Giovanni ed Edoardo rispondono: "Il bosche serve per..." / "Puntare per un po di precisione."

item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Tutor asks many open-ended and reasoning questions (what is a conditional, what will print, how to fix code) plus some closed checks. Approx. 60% open-ended / conceptual, 40% closed or procedural/technical.
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Lesson is highly productive with continuous explanation, coding, debugging, and Kahoot. Technical/admin (login issues, links) and minor dead time are limited. Approx. 85–90% productive learning, 10–15% technical/admin.

item_1_question: Warm-Up and Greetings Did the tutor greet the students in a friendly and engaging manner and explicitly ask at least one small-talk question about the students' week, school, or holidays (e.g., "How was your week?
item_1_why: Tutor greeted warmly but did not ask any small-talk question about students' week, school, or holidays.
item_1_examples: "Hello, hello, good morning, everyone." | "How are you doing today?"
item_1_instead: Tutor moved directly into lesson purpose and homework questions without any personal/small-talk inquiry.
item_2_question: Lesson Objectives In the early part of the lesson (first ~ 25 minutes) did the tutor clearly state the goals of the lesson before starting the practical part of the lesson (e.g., "Today we will learn…", "Today we will discuss…", "In this lesson we are going to explore…", "By the end of this lesson you will be able to…", "The lesson is about…", "The lesson will be about…", "The lesson is dedicated to…", "Today we will cover…")?
item_2_why: Tutor clearly stated what they would learn and do today near the start, before practical work began.
item_2_examples: "Today, we are going to be learning how to create boosts, okay?" | "Today, we are going to be using, again, the click detector, and we are going to create a boost that is going to change the character's speed and the jump height. And we are going to create a few new obstacles, and then we are going to test out our project."
item_3_question: Revision of the Previous Class Did the tutor ask the students to recall what they studied in the previous class by actively involving them at the beginning or early part of the lesson?
item_3_why: Tutor explicitly asked students to recall prior content (GUI, click detector) and students responded with what they remembered.
item_3_examples: "Okay, so tell me, Thaddeus, What does GUI mean?" followed by student typing and tutor confirming: "GUI means Graphical User Interface." | "And Freddie, do you remember what is a click detector?" and Freddie: "I don't remember, but it's, like, on, like, those coat lamp door things."
item_4_question: Homework Review Did the tutor review or ask about the students' home task (even if the word "homework" was not explicitly used), for example by referring to a specific assignment that was meant to be done at home (e.g., "Have you completed your homework assignments?
item_4_why: Tutor opened with homework questions and helped multiple students with specific homework-related tasks.
item_4_examples: "So, first, tell me, are there any questions about homework?" | Discussion with Olivia/Colin: "We struggled with the homework..." and tutor guiding adding Surface GUI and text label. | Helping Vidya: "I tried in the, like, for the homework to do that." and tutor shows how to change text property.
item_5_question: Clarity of explanation: Clear and Manageable Instruction Chunking In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor break instructions into manageable, child-friendly chunks?
item_5_why: Instructions for both scripting and publishing were broken into clear, sequential, child-friendly steps without overloading students.
item_5_examples: "So, click on that part right there. The red one. Okay, and now… Click right here on the plus button, and add the Surface GUI… And now click on the plus button on the Surface GUI. And add the text label." | Publishing: "First, we are going to click on to File. And then, I am going to click on… Publish to Roblox As… When this window pops up, I want you guys to click Create New Game. And here, you can type out the name of the game, and click Create."
item_6_question: Clarity of Explanation: Effective Modeling Before Expecting Independence In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor model the task clearly before expecting students to work independently?
item_6_why: Tutor modeled tasks (boost script behavior, publishing flow) before and while asking students to do them independently, reducing confusion.
item_6_examples: Boost modeling: "So, see, I can click on the ball, because I have added the click detector, and when I click on it… I'm going to move very, very, very fast, and also jump very, very, very high. So, that is how the booster does." | Publishing modeling: "So, how to publish our game. First, we are going to click on to File… click on Publish to Roblox As… When this window pops up…" before giving them time to do it themselves.
item_7_question: Student Engagement (Names) Score 1 only if: - tutor calls at least one student by name more than 2 times during the lesson
item_7_why: Tutor repeatedly used several students’ names throughout the lesson, each more than twice.
item_7_examples: Frequent use of "Olivia" (e.g., "Yes, Olivia?", "Good job, Olivia.", "Olivia, did you have a question?"). | Frequent use of "Weather/Weda" (Vidya) (e.g., "Wenda, you have a question?", "And, Weda, did you add the boost?", "Did you have a question, Veda?"). | Frequent use of "Thaddeus/Sadius" (e.g., "tell me, Thaddeus, What does GUI mean?", "Okay, while Thaddeus is typing out, his answer", "Okay, so, Sadius, turn on your share screen, please.").
item_8_question: Student Engagement (Participation) Score 1 only if tutor directly invites student participation (answer/read/explain/share idea)
item_8_why: Tutor consistently invited students to participate by answering questions, sharing screens, and giving examples.
item_8_examples: "Okay, do you maybe want to share your screen, and I can see how I can help?" | "Who can tell me? What is a boost in a game? So, if you know what's a boost, raise your hand." | "Tell me, Freddie, what was your favorite part about making the Obi game?"

item_13_question: Lesson Wrap-Up (Tutor-led) In the final segment of the lesson (last ~25 minutes), did the tutor deliver a content-based wrap-up by summarizing what was learned or accomplished?
item_13_why: In the final segment, tutor summarized key learning and activities with specific content terms before saying goodbye.
item_13_examples: "So, today we have learned about speed boost, we have learned about which parameter is responsible for the character speed boost, so it is the walk speed. And we have revised one more time how we publish our projects." | "And in the next module, we are going to be making a racing game." (part of closing summary of continuity).

item_17_comment: Tutor: ~75–80% (explaining, modeling, giving step-by-step help). Students collectively: ~20–25% (questions, answers, reflections, troubleshooting while sharing screens). Individual rough: Freddie ~7–8%, Vidya/Weather ~7–8%, Olivia/Colin ~5–6%, Thaddeus mostly via chat ~3–4%.

item_19_comment: Most feedback was procedural or brief ("Okay, great.", "Good job, Olivia.", "So now it is working just fine."). item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?

Approx. 40% open-ended, 60% closed/technical.

item_21_comment: Most of the time was on learning activities: explaining boosts, scripting, designing obstacles, publishing, and troubleshooting. Some time on window resizing/technical navigation. Approx. 85–90% productive learning, 10–15% technical/admin/dead time.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: A substantial portion required thinking (concept of boosts, game design decisions, reflection on difficulty/favorites), but many prompts were procedural (click here, open this, publish that). Approx. 45% thinking/reasoning prompts, 55% procedural/technical prompts.

item_17_question: Comment: Talk Balance (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate the ratio of tutor talk vs. student talk during the lesson. Return as percentages, for example: "Tutor: 74%, Student1: 14%, Student2: 12%"
item_17_comment: Tutor talk dominates but students contribute regularly with answers and questions. Approx: Tutor 75–80%, Kennard 5–7%, Saufa 4–6%, Aqila 4–6%, Shakila 3–5%, Hasna 3–5%, Shanum 3–5%, Shafia ~2–3%.
item_18_question: Comment: Student Engagement Rate (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of students actively engaged (spoke more than twice)?
item_18_comment: Most named students speak multiple times (Kennard, Saufa, Aqila, Shakila, Hasna, Shanum). Roughly 6–7 of about 7–8 students are actively involved: ~80–90% engagement.
item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Feedback is often specific (about text size, color, panel size, using layers) rather than generic praise. Estimated ~70% specific, 30% generic (e.g., "bagus", "nice").
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Many questions are open-ended (opinions, explanations, recalling steps), mixed with some closed/technical ("sudah?", "ada kendala?"). Approx: 60% open-ended, 40% closed/technical.
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Most of the session is on content: reviewing PR, teaching comic concepts, demonstrating Krita, and guiding practice. Some time on platform loading/submission. Approx: 85–90% productive learning, 10–15% technical/admin.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: A good portion of prompts require thinking (recalling prior content, comparing comics vs other images, describing favorite webtoon, deciding format/style). Many are procedural (click here, save, submit). Approx: 45% thinking/reasoning, 55% procedural/technical.

item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Lesson time was highly productive: after brief pet chat and greetings, most time was on math content, guided practice, and platform work. Some admin/tech troubleshooting and movement break. Approx. 85–90% productive learning, 10–15% technical/admin/social time.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts required conceptual reasoning (interpreting negative numbers in contexts, comparing values, explaining operations), alongside procedural tasks (classifying numbers, clicking options). Roughly 55–60% thinking/reasoning prompts, 40–45% procedural or technical prompts.

item_1_question: Warm-Up and Greetings Did the tutor greet the students in a friendly and engaging manner and explicitly ask at least one small-talk question about the students' week, school, or holidays (e.g., "How was your week?
item_1_why: Tutor rozpoczął lekcję uprzejmym powitaniem i nawiązał lekką rozmowę niezwiązaną z technikaliami (o kocie ucznia).
item_1_examples: "Dzień, dobry." | "tam widzę mamy towarzysza kota z tyłu. jak ma na imię?"
item_2_question: Lesson Objectives In the early part of the lesson (first ~ 25 minutes) did the tutor clearly state the goals of the lesson before starting the practical part of the lesson (e.g., "Today we will learn…", "Today we will discuss…", "In this lesson we are going to explore…", "By the end of this lesson you will be able to…", "The lesson is about…", "The lesson will be about…", "The lesson is dedicated to…", "Today we will cover…")?
item_2_why: Cele lekcji zostały jasno i szczegółowo przedstawione na początku, przed przejściem do zadań praktycznych.
item_2_examples: "zaczynamy dzisiaj naszą trzecią lekcję i dzisiaj nauczymy się zbierania informacji... będziemy w stanie tworzyć takie programy, które wchodzą w jakąś interakcję z użytkownikiem" | "To jest jakieś tutaj zbędne, więc nam zostają współrzędne, input Speed, I, Weid? To dzisiaj, te 4 rzeczy poznamy"
item_3_question: Revision of the Previous Class Did the tutor ask the students to recall what they studied in the previous class by actively involving them at the beginning or early part of the lesson?
item_3_why: Na początku lekcji tutor poprosił o przypomnienie wcześniejszych treści, a uczeń aktywnie odpowiadał.
item_3_examples: "to szybka powtórka, co było do tej pory. Gdybyście mieli wytypować Które z tych kafelków już się pojawiły" | Sebastian: "I Down?" ... "Nie ma koloru." | Tutor: "Tak. Zmiana koloru była jak najbardziej"
item_4_question: Homework Review Did the tutor review or ask about the students' home task (even if the word "homework" was not explicitly used), for example by referring to a specific assignment that was meant to be done at home (e.g., "Have you completed your homework assignments?
item_4_why: Tutor wyraźnie odniósł się do zadania domowego, zapytał o problemy i omówił poprawne rozwiązanie drugiego zadania.
item_4_examples: "Sprawdzenie zadania domowego... może ktoś ma jakieś pytania? Czy jakieś coś innego do powiedzenia na temat tego zadania domowego" | "drugie zadanie domowe... jest ktoś, kto to próbował rozwiązać..." | "to może zanim przejdziemy do tego, co na dzisiaj to ja pokażę, jak jak powinno być to zadanie zrobione" (następnie szczegółowo pokazuje rozwiązanie)
item_5_question: Clarity of explanation: Clear and Manageable Instruction Chunking In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor break instructions into manageable, child-friendly chunks?
item_5_why: Instrukcje były konsekwentnie dzielone na małe, wykonalne kroki (pojedyncze linijki kodu, pojedyncze współrzędne, kolejne etapy zadania).
item_5_examples: Przy input: najpierw tworzenie zmiennej, potem dodanie komunikatu, potem osobno print z konkatenacją. | Przy zadaniu z gotu: "mamy podaną konkretną ścieżkę... mamy zacząć od tej współrzędnej... Więc teraz możemy zacząć od tej drugiej" i prowadzenie po jednym go to. | Przy awatarze: każdy krok (tło, twarz, uszy, oczy, nos, usta, włosy) realizowany osobno przez innego ucznia z jasnymi mikroinstrukcjami.
item_6_question: Clarity of Explanation: Effective Modeling Before Expecting Independence In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor model the task clearly before expecting students to work independently?
item_6_why: Zanim uczniowie pracowali samodzielnie, tutor kilkukrotnie modelował zadania na swoim ekranie, pokazując pełne przykłady kodu i działania programów.
item_6_examples: Szczegółowe przejście przez zadanie domowe z datą urodzin: deklaracja zmiennej, kolejne operacje, print i interpretacja wyniku. | Demonstracja funkcji input: tworzenie zmiennej, wpisywanie danych w konsoli, później użycie w print z tekstem. | Przed zadaniami z gotu/speed/wid/dot: wyjaśnienie na slajdach i przykładach, zanim poprosił uczniów o wykonanie zadań na swoich ekranach.
item_7_question: Student Engagement (Names) Score 1 only if: - tutor calls at least one student by name more than 2 times during the lesson
item_7_why: Tutor wielokrotnie zwracał się po imieniu do kilku uczniów, każdy więcej niż dwa razy.
item_7_examples: "Sebastian Zieliński" / "Sebastian" pojawia się wielokrotnie w dialogu. | "Monika" – np. "to może monika...", "dzięki monika", "Monika, to by się udało" itd. | "Zuzia", "Lidia/Liliana", "Janek", "Kuba" – imiona powtarzane przy przydzielaniu zadań i sprawdzaniu postępów.
item_8_question: Student Engagement (Participation) Score 1 only if tutor directly invites student participation (answer/read/explain/share idea)
item_8_why: Tutor systematycznie zapraszał uczniów do aktywnego udziału: czytania poleceń, rozwiązywania zadań, odpowiadania na pytania i zadawania własnych.
item_8_examples: "możesz udostępnić za ekran i spróbować zrobić zadanie" (do Moniki). | "Czy działanie tej funkcji. Jest dla was jasne, czy potrzebujecie jeszcze jakiś wyjaśnień?" | "ktoś jest chętny na ochotnikach, żeby jeszcze usta i włosy zrobić" oraz późniejsze zgłoszenie Liliany.
item_9_question: Broad and Inclusive Participation Did the tutor actively involve multiple students rather than relying on one active child?
item_9_why: Udział w zadaniach był świadomie rozdzielany między wielu uczniów; każdy dostawał swoją część pracy przy awatarze i innych ćwiczeniach.
item_9_examples: Przydzielanie zadań po kolei: "monika...", potem "Zuzia", potem "Lidia", "Janek", "Kuba" przy różnych krokach awatara. | Wspólne zadanie z losowaniem postaci: tutor prosi wielu uczniów o wklejenie wyników na czat (Janek, Monika, Lidia, Kuba). | Quiz na końcu: wszyscy mają dołączyć, tutor sprawdza, kto jeszcze nie dołączył (Janek, Monika).
item_10_question: Student Engagement (Share Screen / Work) Did the tutor ask students to share their screen OR show their work (e.g., "Share your screen", "Show me what you did", "Please share it with me", "Share your project", "Share your drawing")?
item_10_why: Tutor kilkukrotnie prosił uczniów o udostępnienie ekranu i pokazanie pracy.
item_10_examples: "to już wam udostępniam swój ekran" (modelowanie). | "możesz udostępnić za ekran i spróbować zrobić zadanie" (do Moniki). | "Teraz mam tutaj zuzie na liście" ... Zuzia udostępnia ekran; podobnie: "Lidia! Mam już widać ekran.", "Janek, możesz udostępnić ekran.", "Kuba! Możesz zrobić dodatkowe tutaj."
item_11_question: Did the tutor explicitly ask at least one student to turn their camera on (or to keep it on) in order to support attention/participation during the lesson?
item_11_why: W transkrypcji nie ma żadnej prośby o włączenie kamery; jedyne odniesienie do obrazu to komentarz o kocie, ale bez prośby o kamerę.
item_11_examples: "tam widzę mamy towarzysza kota z tyłu. jak ma na imię?" – obserwacja, nie prośba o kamerę.
item_11_instead: Tutor komentował to, co widzi (np. kota w tle), ale nie prosił nikogo o włączenie lub utrzymanie kamery włączonej.
item_12_question: Content delivery: Did the tutor use the platform (e.g., Kodland) or other visual tools to explain assignments or tasks?
item_12_why: Cała lekcja opierała się na pracy w środowisku programistycznym/na platformie, z udostępnianiem ekranu, slajdami i zadaniami online.
item_12_examples: "to już wam udostępniam swój ekran" oraz późniejsze wielokrotne udostępnianie przez uczniów. | "To będzie druga lekcja coś mi się ta platforma się zaczyna wejść. To mamy już" – odniesienie do platformy z zadaniami. | Opis pracy w zadaniach numerowanych (trzecie, czwarte, szóste, ósme zadanie) wykonywanych w interfejsie z konsolą i kodem.
item_13_question: Lesson Wrap-Up (Tutor-led) In the final segment of the lesson (last ~25 minutes), did the tutor deliver a content-based wrap-up by summarizing what was learned or accomplished?
item_13_why: W końcowej części tutor podsumował kluczowe działania i elementy awatara oraz nawiązał do wykorzystanych pojęć.
item_13_examples: "No i jest i mamy czarne włosy, czyli cały awatar, nam powstał. No i właśnie wykorzystaliśmy tego taki kolor oczu, jaki wylosowaliśmy, takie tło, jakie ma ulubione ten pracownik włosy odpowiednio do płci do koloru. Zrobiliśmy więc wszystko się udało" | Przy quizie: "to jest chyba quiz poprzedniej lekcji... mieliście powtórkę..." – odniesienie do treści wcześniejszej lekcji.
item_14_question: Lesson Wrap-Up (Student-led) In the final segment of the lesson (last ~25 minutes) did the tutor ask students to summarize, reflect on, discuss the lesson, or participate in a revision / summary quiz in the final part of the lesson?
item_14_why: Pod koniec lekcji uczniowie uczestniczyli w quizie powtórkowym oraz zadawali pytania o zrozumienie (np. współrzędne), co stanowi formę uczniowskiego podsumowania i refleksji.
item_14_examples: "jak sobie przejdziecie do ósmego zadania. To macie tam taki quiz do zrobienia" – indywidualny quiz na platformie. | Wspólny quiz z linku: tutor omawia wynik 63% i pyta: "Jakieś może macie takie pytania Które byście potrzebowali jakieś tutaj objaśnienia?" | Liliana: "Ja chciałam dopytać. O, ten w tksi Yg!..." – uczeń prosi o wyjaśnienie, tutor rysuje i tłumaczy, a Liliana potwierdza: "Okej? No dobra, rozumiem."
item_15_question: Homework for the Next Class (Lesson Structure / Continuity) Score 1 only if tutor clearly describes what students must do at home (task details). Merely mentioning “homework exists” = 0.Clear and Meaningful Next Step
item_15_why: Tutor jasno opisał zadanie domowe, co dokładnie mają zrobić z awatarem.
item_15_examples: "pracę domową. Macie jedną teraz, czyli właśnie dopracowanie sobie tego... po prostu urozmaicenie takie podkręcanie go można na przykład dorobić. Nie wiem wąsy okulary, szalik czapkę, cokolwiek tam sobie wymyślicie po prostu sobie poeksperymentować i coś do tego awatara dodać."
item_16_question: Upcoming Lesson (Lesson Structure / Continuity) Score 1 only if tutor clearly states the topic/activity of the next lesson (“Next class we will…”). “See you next week” without topic = 0.
item_16_why: Tutor wspomniał tylko, że spotkają się za tydzień; nie podał tematu ani aktywności kolejnej lekcji.
item_16_examples: "no to na dzisiaj tyle. W takim razie za tydzień się widzimy."
item_16_instead: Zamiast zapowiedzi treści kolejnej lekcji, tutor zakończył ogólnym pożegnaniem i przypomnieniem o pracy domowej, bez wskazania, co będzie na następnych zajęciach.
item_17_question: Comment: Talk Balance (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate the ratio of tutor talk vs. student talk during the lesson. Return as percentages, for example: "Tutor: 74%, Student1: 14%, Student2: 12%"
item_17_comment: Szacunkowo: Tutor ok. 70–75% czasu (wyjaśnienia, modelowanie, prowadzenie krok po kroku, organizacja quizu), Sebastian/Janek ok. 10–12%, Monika ok. 5–7%, Zuzia ok. 4–6%, Liliana ok. 5–7%, Kuba ok. 4–6%. Ogólnie: Tutor ~72%, uczniowie 28% łącznie.
item_18_question: Comment: Student Engagement Rate (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of students actively engaged (spoke more than twice)?
item_18_comment: Aktywnie (>=2 wypowiedzi): Sebastian/Janek, Monika, Zuzia, Liliana, Kuba – praktycznie wszyscy obecni uczniowie. Jeśli przyjąć 5 aktywnych z 5 obecnych: 5 z 5 (100%) aktywnie zaangażowanych.
item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Większość feedbacku była raczej konkretna, odnosząca się do kodu i działań (np. poprawa współrzędnych, tabów, koloru, inputu), a nie tylko ogólne „super”. Szacunkowo ok. 70% informacji zwrotnej było szczegółowych (np. wskazanie błędnej linii, brakującego nawiasu, złej współrzędnej), 30% to ogólne pochwały typu „super, działa”.
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Pytania były głównie kierowane na wykonanie konkretnych kroków (proceduralne) lub sprawdzenie zrozumienia („Czy to jest zrozumiałe?”). Otwarte pytania (wymagające wyjaśnienia, refleksji) pojawiały się rzadziej. Szacunkowo: ok. 25–30% pytań otwartych (np. o problemy z zadaniem domowym, o zrozumienie współrzędnych), 70–75% pytań zamkniętych/proceduralnych ("masz?", "udało się?", "możesz udostępnić?", "czy wszystko ok?").
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Większość czasu była produktywna: wyjaśnienia pojęć (input, gotu, współrzędne, speed, wid, dot), rozwiązywanie zadań, wspólne kodowanie, quizy. Niewielka część to kwestie techniczne (problemy z tabletem, logowanie do quizu). Szacunkowo: ok. 88–92% czasu na naukę i ćwiczenia, 8–12% na technikalia/organizację.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Znaczna część poleceń była proceduralna (wpisz to, kliknij run, zmień współrzędne), ale pojawiały się też fragmenty wymagające myślenia (zrozumienie współrzędnych, analiza zadania z datą urodzin, samodzielne dobranie współrzędnych kropek, projektowanie dodatków do awatara). Szacunkowo: ok. 40% wymagało myślenia/rozumowania, 60% było głównie wykonawczo-techniczne.

item_1_question: Warm-Up and Greetings Did the tutor greet the students in a friendly and engaging manner and explicitly ask at least one small-talk question about the students' week, school, or holidays (e.g., "How was your week?
item_1_why: Tutor greeted the student but did not ask any small-talk question about his week, school, or holidays.
item_1_examples: "Esteban. Buenos días. ¿me escucha. Hola. Hola. Hola." | "Sebastian: Hola. Profe."
item_1_instead: Tutor focused on greeting and technical setup (camera, entering Minecraft) and then immediately asked about the previous class, not about the student’s life or week.
item_2_question: Lesson Objectives In the early part of the lesson (first ~ 25 minutes) did the tutor clearly state the goals of the lesson before starting the practical part of the lesson (e.g., "Today we will learn…", "Today we will discuss…", "In this lesson we are going to explore…", "By the end of this lesson you will be able to…", "The lesson is about…", "The lesson will be about…", "The lesson is dedicated to…", "Today we will cover…")?
item_2_why: Tutor clearly and explicitly stated the lesson objective at the very beginning before starting practical work.
item_2_examples: "La lección de hoy, que es la lección número 6 de nuestro primer módulo." | "¿qué es lo que vamos a estar haciendo Durante la lección de hoy? Sebastián el día de hoy? Vamos a estar construyendo una granja automatizada." | "Vamos a estar construyendo una granja automatizada de que de bambú."
item_3_question: Revision of the Previous Class Did the tutor ask the students to recall what they studied in the previous class by actively involving them at the beginning or early part of the lesson?
item_3_why: Tutor explicitly asked about the previous class and the student responded, showing recall/connection to prior learning.
item_3_examples: "Cuéntame cómo te fue la clase anterior con el profe." | Sebastian: "Bien." / "Todo correcto." | Later: "Te acuerdas cuando nosotros cubrimos un cofre como que hicimos un escondite con un cofre. Lo trabajamos con un pistón pegajoso" and student shows understanding.
item_4_question: Homework Review Did the tutor review or ask about the students' home task (even if the word "homework" was not explicitly used), for example by referring to a specific assignment that was meant to be done at home (e.g., "Have you completed your homework assignments?
item_4_why: Tutor mentioned assignments and tasks in general but did not review or ask about any specific homework from the previous class.
item_4_examples: "No se te olvide hacer el envío de las asignaciones y de las tareas" (end of class).
item_4_instead: Tutor only referenced assignments generically at the end and never asked what he had done at home or checked any prior home task.
item_5_question: Clarity of explanation: Clear and Manageable Instruction Chunking In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor break instructions into manageable, child-friendly chunks?
item_5_why: Instructions are consistently broken into small, sequential, child-friendly steps, with checks for completion before moving on.
item_5_examples: "Quiero que hagas una línea de 10 bloques de tierra... Me avisas cuando lo hayas hecho." Then only after: "Vamos a poner un bloque de agua en una esquina... Esos 2 bloques sin agua, los rompemos." | Teletransport: explains command, sends coordinate, asks him to copy/paste, then waits: "me avisas cuando lo hayas hecho". | Tolva setup: "Primero que todo agacharme... apuntando al lateral del cofre... le doy clic derecho... Quiero que lo hagas y me confirmes."
item_6_question: Clarity of Explanation: Effective Modeling Before Expecting Independence In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor model the task clearly before expecting students to work independently?
item_6_why: Tutor thoroughly modeled each new task (teleporting, pistons, observers, farm layout) on his own screen before asking the student to replicate independently.
item_6_examples: "Mira mi pantalla... Si yo en el chat de Minecraft ingreso una coordenada... al yo, darle enter lo que va a pasar es que esta coordenada me va a teletransportar." Then student repeats. | Pistón: demonstrates pushing blocks and cutting flowers/bambú before asking student to build the mechanism. | Observador + redstone: builds full example, shows how growth triggers pistons, then asks student to recreate: "adelante, señor. Quiero que también lo lo realices."
item_7_question: Student Engagement (Names) Score 1 only if: - tutor calls at least one student by name more than 2 times during the lesson
item_7_why: Tutor repeatedly uses the student’s name throughout the lesson, well over two times.
item_7_examples: "Sebas" and "Sebastián" are used frequently: "Muy bien. Entonces, sin más dilación, sebas.", "por favor, Sebastián, vamos a ir a Minecraft Education.", "Me avisas sebas cuando hayas hecho, la línea"
item_8_question: Student Engagement (Participation) Score 1 only if tutor directly invites student participation (answer/read/explain/share idea)
item_8_why: Tutor frequently invites the student to act, answer, explain, and share ideas, ensuring active participation.
item_8_examples: "Cuéntame cómo te fue la clase anterior con el profe." | "¿para qué se usa el bambú en la vida real?" and later "Sabes, o tienes alguna idea de para qué se usa en Minecraft?" | Multiple procedural prompts: "Me avisas cuando lo hayas hecho", "Explícamelo. Por favor.", "Hasta aquí hay alguna pregunta de cómo funciona el observador Sebas."
item_9_question: Broad and Inclusive Participation Did the tutor actively involve multiple students rather than relying on one active child?
item_9_why: Only one student (Sebastián) is present and engaged; there is no opportunity or evidence of involving multiple students.
item_9_examples: All interactions are between tutor and Sebastián; no other names or voices appear.
item_9_instead: The tutor effectively engaged the single student but could not distribute participation across multiple learners because there were no others in the session.
item_10_question: Student Engagement (Share Screen / Work) Did the tutor ask students to share their screen OR show their work (e.g., "Share your screen", "Show me what you did", "Please share it with me", "Share your project", "Share your drawing")?
item_10_why: Tutor explicitly asked the student to share his screen multiple times to check work and troubleshoot.
item_10_examples: "A ver con parte de pantallas nuevas. Por favor." | "Dale con parte de pantalla si ya está funcionando. Sí, adelante." | "Listo. Muy bien, compártame pantalla por forzar para corroborar que esté funcionando esa parte."
item_11_question: Did the tutor explicitly ask at least one student to turn their camera on (or to keep it on) in order to support attention/participation during the lesson?
item_11_why: Tutor discussed his own camera but never requested the student to turn on or keep on their camera.
item_11_examples: "Regálame un momento y ya enciendo la cámara" (tutor about his own camera).
item_11_instead: Tutor only referenced his own camera setup and did not mention the student’s camera status or ask him to turn it on.
item_12_question: Content delivery: Did the tutor use the platform (e.g., Kodland) or other visual tools to explain assignments or tasks?
item_12_why: Tutor clearly used Minecraft Education (and likely screen share) as the main visual tool to demonstrate concepts and tasks.
item_12_examples: "por favor, Sebastián, vamos a ir a Minecraft Education." | "Quiero que por favor, observen mi pantalla..." followed by demonstrations of teleport, pistons, observers, and farm layout. | Continuous references to what appears on his shared Minecraft screen while explaining mechanics.

item_17_question: Comment: Talk Balance (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate the ratio of tutor talk vs. student talk during the lesson. Return as percentages, for example: "Tutor: 74%, Student1: 14%, Student2: 12%"
item_17_comment: Tutor fala a maior parte, mas há participação frequente de dois alunos e alguma de um terceiro. Estimativa: Tutor ~70%, Lucas ~15%, Eduardo ~13%, Ian ~2%.
item_18_question: Comment: Student Engagement Rate (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of students actively engaged (spoke more than twice)?
item_18_comment: Pelo menos 2 de 3 alunos (Lucas e Eduardo) falam muitas vezes (perguntas, respostas, comentários). Ian participa por chat/compartilhamento, mas menos. Aproximadamente: 2 of 3 students actively involved (66.7%).
item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: A maior parte do feedback é específico e ligado ao código ou raciocínio (explica erros de variável, espaços, concatenação). Pouco elogio genérico. Estimativa: ~80% específico, ~20% genérico ("Muito bem", "Boa").
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Muitas perguntas são abertas ou de raciocínio (como resolver espaços, por que há erro, qual tarefa foi mais difícil). Há também perguntas fechadas de checagem. Estimativa: ~60% abertas, 40% fechadas/administrativas.
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Houve algum tempo com questões técnicas (Zoom, música, ruído) e organização, mas a maior parte foi explicação, prática guiada, quiz e revisão. Estimativa: ~85% produtivo aprendizado, 15% técnico/admin/música-ajuste.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Muitas solicitações exigem pensar (resolver problemas de código, ajustar cálculos, entender operadores, responder quiz conceitual). Também há instruções procedimentais (clicar, compartilhar tela). Estimativa: ~65% pensamento/raciocínio, 35% procedimentos técnicos.

item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"

Estimate ~55% thinking/reasoning prompts (predicting behavior, recalling methods, designing conditions/messages), 45% procedural/technical (click here, copy this, restart Roblox).

item_21_comment: Most of the session is active teaching, modeling, coding, and debugging with students. Some time is spent on a short break and minor audio/monitor issues. Approx ~88% productive learning time, ~12% technical/admin/break time.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts require reasoning about scripts, properties, welding behavior, and design choices, but there are also many procedural "click here, change this" instructions. Approx ~45% thinking/reasoning prompts vs 55% procedural/technical prompts.

item_2_question: Lesson Objectives In the early part of the lesson (first ~ 25 minutes) did the tutor clearly state the goals of the lesson before starting the practical part of the lesson (e.g., "Today we will learn…", "Today we will discuss…", "In this lesson we are going to explore…", "By the end of this lesson you will be able to…", "The lesson is about…", "The lesson will be about…", "The lesson is dedicated to…", "Today we will cover…")?
item_2_why: Tutor clearly stated what they would do and learn at the start, before practical activities.
item_2_examples: "Muito bom ter vocês aqui novamente. A gente vai começar uma aulinha. Hoje vamos aprender várias coisas novas. E também vamos fazer alguns joguinhos, mas antes disso..." | "Disso, a gente vai ajudar o nosso ursinho Ted a abrir o baú."
item_3_question: Revision of the Previous Class Did the tutor ask the students to recall what they studied in the previous class by actively involving them at the beginning or early part of the lesson?
item_3_why: No explicit prompt for students to recall previous lesson content; only a passing reference to doing something similar before.
item_3_examples: "A número dois. Pessoal, é uma corrida no teclado. A gente fez igual na passada."
item_3_instead: Tutor referenced the previous class herself but did not ask students what they remembered or have them demonstrate prior knowledge; students did not describe last lesson’s content.
item_4_question: Homework Review Did the tutor review or ask about the students' home task (even if the word "homework" was not explicitly used), for example by referring to a specific assignment that was meant to be done at home (e.g., "Have you completed your homework assignments?
item_4_why: Tutor explicitly assigned unfinished tasks as home activity and framed them as to be done at home.
item_4_examples: "Quem não acabou o joguinho vai ficar de atividade de casa. Tá bom." | "Pessoal, prestem atenção aqui. Quem não conseguiu terminar? Vocês podem terminar de atividade de casa. Tudo bem." | "Essas atividades que a gente fez hoje, vocês vão treinar durante a semana na casa de vocês. Tá bom?"
item_5_question: Clarity of explanation: Clear and Manageable Instruction Chunking In the middle part of the lesson (from ~ minute 25 to minute ~50) did the tutor break instructions into manageable, child-friendly chunks?
item_5_why: Instructions for tasks were broken into small, sequential steps with clear next actions, especially during platform activities.

item_17_question: Comment: Talk Balance (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate the ratio of tutor talk vs. student talk during the lesson. Return as percentages, for example: "Tutor: 74%, Student1: 14%, Student2: 12%"
item_17_comment: Tutor: ~75–80% (long explanations, technical guidance, modeling). Students collectively: ~20–25% (short answers, questions, chatter; Aurora and Arthur speak most, others less).
item_18_question: Comment: Student Engagement Rate (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of students actively engaged (spoke more than twice)?
item_18_comment: There appear to be about 6–7 students (Aurora, Arthur, Ícaro, Ana/Analice, Lara, Daniel, possibly Kelly). At least Aurora, Arthur, Ícaro, Ana, Lara, and Daniel speak multiple times. Roughly 5–6 of 6–7 students actively involved: about 70–85% engagement.
item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Most feedback is generic praise ("Muito bem", "Perfeito", "Parabéns"). Occasionally slightly specific ("Muito bem, Daniel, pode clicar na setinha verde pra ir pra próxima"), but detail is low. Approx. 80–90% generic, 10–20% specific.
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Tutor asks many open content questions ("Quem sabe pra que serve a barra de espaço?", "O que a gente faz com esse gatinho primeiro?", "Vocês acharam difícil ou acharam fácil?") but also many closed/technical ones ("Está todo mundo conseguindo me ouvir?", "Vocês estão conseguindo ver minha tela?"). Rough estimate: ~55–60% open-ended/content questions, ~40–45% closed/technical.
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: A large portion is productive (concept explanation, guided practice, platform navigation). Some time is lost to waiting for students to join and technical troubleshooting (screens, links, Zoom navigation). Approx. 75–80% productive learning, 20–25% technical/admin/dead time.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts require thinking (explaining what keyboard parts do, planning sequences of actions, deciding moves in labirinto/esquilo tasks). However, a lot of instructions are procedural (click here, share screen, recarregar página). Roughly 50–55% thinking/reasoning prompts, 45–50% procedural/technical prompts.

item_21_comment: Most of the lesson is active math instruction, guided practice, and platform-based problem solving. Some admin/tech time around platform login and screen sharing. Approx: 85–90% productive learning, 10–15% technical/admin/dead time.

item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Rough estimate: ~60% of feedback was somewhat specific (e.g., "Muito bom", tied to correct tool choice or action like "Desenhar muito bem, porque a gente quer desenhar uma ponte"), ~40% generic praise or neutral acknowledgments ("Show de bola", "Boa", "Beleza").
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?

item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: About 75–80% productive learning time (platform navigation, tool use, building bridge, story context, homework explanation, reflection). Around 20–25% technical/admin/dead time (login issues, Roblox Studio bugs, waiting for loading, short break).
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Approximately 45% prompts required thinking/reasoning (choosing tools, predicting outcomes, describing what they see, reflecting on learning), while ~55% were procedural/technical (click here, open file, enter password, follow steps).

item_17_question: Comment: Talk Balance (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate the ratio of tutor talk vs. student talk during the lesson. Return as percentages, for example: "Tutor: 74%, Student1: 14%, Student2: 12%"
item_17_comment: Tutor talk ~65–70%. Akın ~10–12%. Bartu ~10–12%. Harun ~6–8%. Ahmet Toprak ~5–7%. Others (Kaan, Metehan, Taha, Can Deniz) together ~3–5%.
item_18_question: Comment: Student Engagement Rate (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of students actively engaged (spoke more than twice)?
item_18_comment: Class size around 7–8. Actively engaged (multiple turns) include Akın, Ahmet Toprak, Bartu, Harun; possibly 4 of 7–8 ≈ 50–57% actively involved.
item_19_question: Comment: Feedback Specificity (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of feedback instances were detailed vs. generic praise?
item_19_comment: Feedback is often specific (around 70%) vs generic (30%). Examples: "Harika. Kesinlikle kişisel bilgilerimizi içermemeli..." and detailed praise of Akın’s virus-removal steps, versus generic "Süper" or "Harikasın" without elaboration.
item_20_question: Comment: Question Quality (Quantitative - not scored) What percentage of tutor questions were open-ended vs. closed (yes/no, technical)?
item_20_comment: Tutor questions are mostly open-ended conceptual or reflective (about 70%) such as "Peki böyle bir durumda ne yapmalıyız?" and "Var mı başka fikir üretmek isteyen"; about 30% are closed/administrative (age, city, yes/no comfort, etc.).
item_21_question: Comment: Productive Time Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of the lesson was spent on productive learning activity vs technical/admin/dead time. Return as, for example: "About 82% productive learning time, 18% technical/admin/dead time"
item_21_comment: Productive learning time is high; minimal technical/admin. Approx. 85–90% on content discussion (internet safety, hacking, viruses, OS, coding paths), 10–15% on attendance, introductions, and logistics.
item_22_question: Comment: Cognitive Demand Ratio (Quantitative - not scored) Estimate what percentage of tutor prompts required student thinking/reasoning vs simple procedural compliance. Return as, for example: "About 42% thinking/reasoning prompts, 58% procedural or technical prompts"
item_22_comment: Many prompts require reasoning and sharing experiences (internet safety decisions, handling threats, evaluating messages) vs some procedural/biographical questions. Roughly 60% thinking/reasoning prompts, 40% procedural or factual (age, city, course info, logistics).

Rough estimate: 35–40% thinking/reasoning (linking images to concepts, metacognition explanation, end-of-class reflections), 60–65% procedural/technical guidance.
